speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 9, 1996. Survivor is Rita Finver, F-I-N-V-E-R. The interviewer's Andrew Hartmann, H-A-R-T-M-A-N-N. The city is Tamarac in the Miami, Florida district, USA. The language is English, and this is roll number 1. My name is Andrew Hartmann, H-A-R-T-M-A-N-N. Today's date is January 9, 1996. We will be conducting an interview with Rita Finver, F-I-N-V-E-R, in Tamarac, Florida in the USA. Would you please tell us your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rita Finver, R-I-T-A F-I-V-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Finver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: F-I-N-V-E--

speaker: interviewee
text: V, like Victor, E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: E-R. And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rita Wielopolska. It's a typical Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Wielopolska for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-E-L-O-P-O-L-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth? And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 8, 1920. I just had my birthday yesterday-- birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Happy birthday. And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 76 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country where you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lesko, Poland, which is the old Galicia in the Carpathian Mountains, the southern part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family, starting with your parents, brothers, and sisters? Tell me their names, your father's occupation, your mother's occupation, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother-- this was the second marriage for the two of them. But they never spoke about it, because there were no children prior to-- with the first marriage. I was born when my mother, apparently, was, was over 30. She divorced her first husband because they didn't have children. And according to the Jewish law, if you don't have children, you're entitled to divorce each other. We were five children at home. I was the oldest. I was the oldest on both sides, from my maternal grandfather and paternal grandfather. The paternal grandfather and my father were born in Brzozow, Brzozow, also Poland, which was maybe 30 kilometers from our town. We were five children at, at home. After-- I was the oldest. Then was a brother, Abraham. There was a brother, David. There was a sister, [NON-ENGLISH], and there was the youngest one, which was called [NON-ENGLISH]. Our grandfather-- the maternal grandfather-- lived, we lived in the same building. He was ultra-Orthodox, and so was grandmother. They were strictly-- very strict in, in their religious behavior. And even though my parents were also Orthodox and kosher-- but grandfather was so strict that he didn't even want to eat in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name and occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Naftali Wielopolski. When he came to our town when I was a young child, he was buying skins from wild animals or from rabbits. And these skins were dried. And then it was bought by a middle distributor and sent, apparently, to United States to make coats. But this was pretty, a pretty good income until the, the Depression started in 1930, when the entire export was cut off. By that time, my mother, who had a candy store during the First World War in the beginning of '20s but somehow it was taken away from her, she was trying to get a store in the same building square, which was a very prominent square. And she did get a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened a grocery store. The grocery store was opened approximately 1927 or '28. So my father at that time and mother were already staying in the store. Was your father a, a veteran of the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he serve?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a veteran of the First World War. And until he got married-- until he married Mother-- he was, I would say, a observant Jew but rather on the modern side. He was not wearing dark, very dark clothes or, or velvet clothes. When he married Mother, he really adapted to the more Orthodox way of life. He was, during the war-- as a child, I remember now, during the war in Sarajevo. And, and so I, I-- it also comes to my mind that he always talked that he was there in Sarajevo. He was sent there when the war started. He was also-- before he married Mother, he was in Germany. I don't know exactly in which place. But he had one or two pictures where he was dressed in, I'd say, modern gray suit clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily life like in your family, in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest in our home. And until I started school at the age of 5, I was brought up where my father was born. My father was born in Przysietnica near Brzozow. And I was there, pampered by my paternal grandfather and by all aunts and uncles who were still single at that time. So I really had a very lovely childhood. After my brother was born-- and Mother was always busy. She kept, was kept busy at home. And she was always dealing with something when the business was not very good. She was providing milk. She was buying milk and butter from peasants and selling it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody came to help with, let's say, my brother, who was after me, Abraham. Or grandmother, who lived in the same house, came sort of to help out and babysit. When I was older, when I came home and there were already five children, I even remember that Mother gave me the responsibility of taking care of the youngest to diaper her or to, to put her to sleep in a little wooden-- what do you call that? A little cradle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother was already at that time older, and she couldn't always, let's say, take care. So I had responsibilities very frequently at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a city or town was Lesko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lesko was a town-- of course, there was never a census-- but approximately there were 2,000 Polish and Ukrainian people. And there were between 4,000 and 5,000 Jews. The Jews were predominantly small merchants-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how shall I say-- tailors, cabinet makers, trade. They trade. The Gentiles were always on the, the official, the officials in the offices. And Jews hardly ever-- we had, I think, two Jewish doctors, which nobody even knew that they were Jewish, because they were not associated with the Jewish. They didn't go to the synagogue. And there was one or two Jewish lawyers. Also, they were very progressive. They were really not Orthodox or in the midstream of, of Orthodox Jewish life. It was a predominantly Orthodox city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community life like? Were there organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had organizations when-- the youth was always dragged in into Zionist organization. The Zionist movement was very, very pronounced and very strong. From Mizrahi and Orthodox, to-- from the extreme right to the extreme left, like Hashomer Hatzair, Hashomer Hadati, which were left. And the youth, even though they grew up in Orthodox home, was more modern. They did not observe Saturday Shabbat as, let's say, our parents would, would expect it from us. Even-- I did not belong to organization because I left our town at the age of 13. But while or before 13, I was never wearing at home, let's say, a dress with sleeves shorter above the, the elbow. It was always below. This was the influence of my very ultra-Orthodox grandfather. Saturday, if Father saw-- even Father, who was brought up in a more progressive home, if he saw me combing my hair or, or polishing my shoes, this was, let's say, taboo because it was Shabbat. So it was really a very, very strict Orthodox upbringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall approximately how many synagogues there were in Lesko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived, we lived in the Jewish neighborhood. The Jewish neighborhood, we were-- our house-- next door to our house was a-- it was called a Sadigura shtiebel. The names were usually after the type of Hasidim who, who believed in a certain rabbi. The Sadigura was a sect. And my father was a Sadigura [NON-ENGLISH]. The Sadigura rabbi was a more intelligent and more progressive-- I understand that he was born in Vienna during the Austrian, you know, time. And he had perhaps some education. He was more modern. My grandfather was a Chortkiv rabbi. Chortkiv was somewhere on the eastern part of Poland already, I believe in, in, in-- I don't know exactly where Chortkiv was. But Chortkiv, I think, was near-- further than Stanislawow and Tarnopol, close to the Romanian border. And my grandfather was very Orthodox. There were other little, little shtiebel, I would say, little synagogues. All of them had at least 400 or 500 people praying at one time. We had a very old, prominent synagogue, which was very-- the structure was very old but yet very solid. It was 300, minimum 300 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our town really started, had a history of about 300 or more years of existence-- of Jewish, of Jewish population existence. Very possible that after the Spanish Inquisition, when there was a movement from Spain into, let's say, Germany and part of them were accepted by the Polish ruler, who was at that time accepting the Jews into Poland. And this is when the Jews settled in our town. Do any of these synagogues remain today, as far as you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my knowledge, the entire Jewish city-- the entire-- not the city, but the entire Jewish part of the city-- was destroyed except for the big synagogue, where some of the chandeliers were probably taken out and other things. But now it's a museum. The Germans did not destroy, destroy this synagogue. It was so built-- the walls were so thick, they would have-- they probably could not destroy it anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is left. And there is now a museum. And even if somebody goes to the city, they go to it. If somebody goes back to Poland to our city, they go to see the museum-- whatever's left there. What was your experience? Or did you experience any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really personally didn't feel it. Maybe it was because of my Polish name, also because my childhood was spent with my paternal grandfather, and I spoke a very good Polish. My Polish didn't have the Jewish accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So unless-- of course, there was no secret. Everybody knew that I was Jewish. In our schools, in the public schools, even though the schools were open on Saturday and they were teaching on Saturday subjects-- but the teachers-- since we had-- 80% of the students were Jewish, so the teachers usually gave the secondary subjects like gym, arts and crafts.

speaker: interviewee
text: These, these subjects were taught on Saturday. But we were compelled to take the guidelines from other students and make it up on our own. So the next week or in the middle of the week if you had a art class, we had to bring our, our projects prepared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The schools, the schools really were, I would say, on a very high level. Starting from the second grade, since we had quite large Ukrainian population in the outskirts of the city, we were taught the, the Ukrainian alphabet, which is also the same alphabet as in, as was used in Russia. We were taught from second grade on. On the fifth grade, we were taught German. At the beginning, they even taught the Gothic, which was later abandoned. So we were taught German. And even towards the end-- it was a seventh-grade school. But towards the end, it was already like two or three years in, let's say, a high school. It was like a junior high school in United States. I would presume so. I don't know exactly how the curriculum here is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely, only Yiddish, but-- and even on the street, mostly with my friends, I spoke a lot of time Jewish. But the Polish-- the more modern and if some-- well, we used Polish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first-- you and your family-- first experience signs of any danger, and what were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I, I really cannot say exactly, because we felt it in our town. We felt because there were some uprisings at one time. But I wasn't home at that time. And as a kid, my parents didn't talk to me about it. So I really never heard any fear or expression from, from them. When I was older already, at that time, I was not home. I, I was coming home for vacations or for holidays. But I really cannot say that they were talking about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollection of any particular events, let's say, in 1937, '38, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not at our town at that time. I left our town. I was 13 years old. And I went to Oswiecim, which is called Auschwitz. But it was the town of Oswiecim. It was not the concentration camp. We had there relatives. And they treated me very well. And I went to Katowice. They had a store there. I worked in their store. And I was-- for some holidays, I come home. But most of the time, I spend already with these relatives. This was like my second family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first have experience with foreign troops? Were you in the part of Poland where the Russians came in or the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was in Katowice September 1, 1939, when the war started. I was living in a building which had six or, six or eight stories high. I don't remember exactly. And I had there a room, which I shared with another girl my, my age. When the Germans came in, I heard it on the radio. In Katowice, there was not one shot. I didn't see one German the first day. But when I went down on the street, all the windows-- in every window was Hitler's portrait and with the sign, Heil Hitler. So even though they were tolerant and I never heard it from them prior to September 1931, they were all Volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: Volksdeutsche-- meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: They waited for, for the Germans to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was with the Germans-- yeah, the first day of war, the first day of war since it was-- nothing was destroyed. And I saw what was going on. So I went on a train. And I went from Katowice to Oswiecim, where the owner of the store lived. The owner of the store was our cousin, a Orthodox Jew, also with a beard and with a quite Orthodox lifestyle. I came to them because I knew Saturday the store was closed anyhow. And it was that. And I was sort of-- I didn't know what to do. So I took the train. I went to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, I spend a Saturday with them. And Saturday, Sunday morning, when they heard that the German is, is going in very deep into the Polish territory, they decided to take a train and run away. Where? Wherever the train takes them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train from Oswiecim went to Cracow. So I went with them. They had five children. I, of course, was older than their oldest daughter. When we came halfway, we were near-- it was a town which was called Skawina, where they were making-- there were some factories there. We were-- from a helicopter or from a light plane, they started to shoot on us. And we had to jump off the train and lie down in the fields. Nobody was killed at that time from us. But it was quite an experience. And maybe after half an hour, when the plane went away, we went back on the train. That was a cattle train. The passenger trains were not available anymore. From there, we arrived to Cracow. And the, the cousin-- I mean, the head of the family-- he rented a little room in-- it was called Podgorze in Cracow. It was a Jewish section, and somebody either rented or just let him in. And we were there about two weeks until everybody knew already that the Germans are quite deep in the Polish territory. And there was talk that perhaps we should go back. It's time to go back to Oswiecim to see what-- if there is a place to go back to. I went together with two others not from the family with whom I was staying or for whom I was working, but their cousins. It was a bigger family in Oswiecim. So with two other girls-- I don't remember how we went back. The trains were running. It was not really destroyed. There was no destruction in this section of Poland. So we came back to Oswiecim, Oswiecim, which normally was a city of, I think, of about 10,000 Jews approximately. I don't know exactly the numbers. It was empty. There was only one store that I saw. A bakery was open, and he started to make bread. But little by little bit, people started to trickle down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, one of us-- we were three girls-- we came. I remain already there. One of them went and gave them the signal-- there was no telephone connection-- gave them the signal that if they want to, they can come home, that the homes were not destroyed. This was in September in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately from the 1st of September, probably until maybe the third week in September, I came back to Oswiecim. When I came back, of course, since the store was in Katowice, our-- the owner of the store, which was our cousin, he-- since he was a bearded man, a, a man with a-- a Jew with a beard and with, with Orthodox attire-- so of course, I was the one who went back. His children-- his oldest daughter was four years younger than I was. She was too young to do anything to help them. I went back to Katowice. And I was in Katowice September, October, November-- September-- the end of September-- September, October, November. During that time in Katowice was a Jewish committee, which was on the [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the Jewish-- this was their building from before the war, too. They have made, like, a soup kitchen for the people who couldn't get food. They were coordinating a lot of things. During that period of time was the first deportation of all able young Jewish men. They took them together. And the Jewish committee gave a request that whoever has stores with clothing or with shovels or anything like that, that these stores should provide these things voluntarily. If they will not give voluntarily, the Germans will just break the, the lock and go in and get whatever they need. So I was at that time there, too. And since it was a clothing store, I provided some of the clothing for the men. During that deportation, none of the wife or children knew where the men were going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans said that they are going somewhere to a labor, labor place-- place of labor-- that they will work there. And the wives will later find out where they went. I remain in Katowice for, as I said, to the end or beginning of December. I remained there until I noticed that since the town was already like a ghost town and I felt that there is no place for me to remain, and even when I-- I was able to take a train from Katowice to Oswiecim. I had a German friend my own age, who was very nice. And since my name was Wielopolska, she made out for me a pass. So I was able to go. And the trains were running between Katowice and Oswiecim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went several times to Oswiecim. I went there for Saturday, usually. And I was talking over with the family what to do. They, at that time, had no other place to go. This was their home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, myself, since I saw that there is a trend of a lot of young people who, perhaps, had some left leanings-- leaned, maybe, you know, socialist-- that a lot of them felt that the German territory's too dangerous. It's better to go to the Russian territory. It was a trend. I, myself, didn't have this in mind. But my parents were there. And I felt my function is finished. Whatever I had to do and whatever, however I could help these relatives while we were running together away, that now is my time to go back to my parents. While I was in Katowice during these three years or four years-- no, more than four years-- I was, besides work, I was trying to study because in our town, Lesko-- Lesko didn't have a gymnasium. Because it was such an Orthodox town, even if there were a high school, my parents would not allow me to go on Saturday. And in a high school, it was compulsory to go on a Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I studied privately in my spare time. And beginning in December 1939, I decided to leave the family in Oswiecim and go to my parents. My parents live in Lesko, which was at the River San but just on the eastern bank of the River San. Our town was on the boundary. On the western side were the Germans, and on the eastern side, Lesko was situated. When I decided to go home, I took along my personal belonging. Also, I had some money-- not too much. But since I was working and, and I was thrifty, I had maybe 200 or 300 zloty, which was not too much money. But at that time were already rumors that on the Russian side items are very expensive, like watches, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cigarette lighter. Cigarette lighter-- the little stones that you put in cigarette lighter. These were small items.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I spent 200 or 300 zloty, and I bought these items, which are two small packages. And I took off home. The trains were already running. This part of Poland was really not, not destroyed. Perhaps Warsaw was destroyed, and I was never in Warsaw at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this part-- the trains were not running normally but slowly. It took me until I came to a town, Sanok. Sanok was on, on the western part of the River San. When I came there from, from our, from the train, I had to take like a little-- with a horse and buggy to go somewhere. And a man came-- a Ukrainian or a Polish man. I don't know exactly what he was. But he came, and he picked me up. And he recognized me. Oh, I recognize you. I used to come to your parents' during-- every Tuesday was like a fair, a county fair. And the peasants were bringing there their food, like their products, I would say-- eggs, butter, cheeses, chickens. They were bringing them to town. And the peasant-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop for a moment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and change the tape in the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
